title: Building Agent Skills Tests: Maximize Your Verification Score
excerpt: Learn how to write tests that maximize your AgentNode verification score. Covers test structure, unit vs integration tests, mock strategies, verification pipeline internals, and tips for reaching Gold tier.
seo_title: Agent Skill Testing Guide: Maximize Verification Score
seo_description: How to write tests that maximize your AgentNode verification score. Test structure, mocking, Gold tier tips, and common pitfalls explained.
content_html:
Publishing an agent skill on AgentNode is straightforward. Publishing one that earns a Gold verification tier is a different challenge entirely. The difference between Bronze and Gold almost always comes down to one thing: your test suite. The verification pipeline analyzes your tests for coverage, correctness, edge-case handling, and determinism — and each dimension contributes to your final score.

This guide walks you through every aspect of writing tests that maximize your verification score. You will learn the test structure the pipeline expects, when to use unit tests versus integration tests, how to mock API-dependent tools effectively, and the specific patterns that push a skill from Silver to Gold.

How the Verification Pipeline Scores Your Tests

Before writing a single test, you need to understand what the pipeline actually measures. The verification system evaluates five dimensions:

  • Coverage — What percentage of your skill's code paths are exercised by tests
  • Correctness — Do tests assert meaningful outcomes, not just that code runs without errors
  • Edge-case handling — Are boundary conditions, empty inputs, and error states tested
  • Determinism — Do tests produce the same results on every run without depending on external state
  • Isolation — Do tests avoid side effects that could affect other tests or the host environment

Each dimension is scored on a 0-100 scale. Your overall verification score is the weighted average, with coverage and correctness weighted most heavily. To understand why this scoring matters for consumers of your skill, read about why verification matters in the AgentNode ecosystem.

Test File Structure

The verification pipeline expects a specific project layout. If your files are not in the right place, the pipeline cannot find them — and your score drops to zero before analysis even begins.

my-agent-skill/
├── src/
│   ├── __init__.py
│   ├── main.py          # Your skill entry point
│   ├── handlers.py      # Request handlers
│   └── utils.py         # Helper functions
├── tests/
│   ├── __init__.py
│   ├── conftest.py      # Shared fixtures
│   ├── test_main.py     # Tests for main.py
│   ├── test_handlers.py # Tests for handlers.py
│   └── test_utils.py    # Tests for utils.py
├── anp.yaml             # Skill manifest
└── pyproject.toml

Key requirements:

  • Tests must live in a tests/ directory at the project root
  • Test files must follow the test_*.py naming convention
  • Each source module should have a corresponding test module
  • A conftest.py file should contain shared fixtures

The pipeline runs pytest under the hood. Any configuration that works with pytest — markers, parametrize, fixtures — works with the verification pipeline.

Unit Tests: The Foundation of Your Score

Unit tests form the backbone of a high verification score. They test individual functions and methods in isolation, making them fast, deterministic, and easy to reason about.

Testing Input Validation

Every agent skill receives input from an AI agent. That input might be malformed, incomplete, or contain unexpected types. Testing input validation is one of the highest-impact things you can do for your score.

import pytest
from src.handlers import validate_input, SkillInput


class TestInputValidation:
    """Tests for input validation logic."""

    def test_valid_input_passes(self):
        data = {"query": "search for Python tools", "limit": 10}
        result = validate_input(data)
        assert result.query == "search for Python tools"
        assert result.limit == 10

    def test_missing_required_field_raises(self):
        with pytest.raises(ValueError, match="query is required"):
            validate_input({"limit": 10})

    def test_empty_query_raises(self):
        with pytest.raises(ValueError, match="query must not be empty"):
            validate_input({"query": "", "limit": 10})

    def test_negative_limit_raises(self):
        with pytest.raises(ValueError, match="limit must be positive"):
            validate_input({"query": "test", "limit": -1})

    def test_limit_defaults_to_twenty(self):
        result = validate_input({"query": "test"})
        assert result.limit == 20

    def test_extra_fields_ignored(self):
        result = validate_input({"query": "test", "unknown_field": "value"})
        assert result.query == "test"
        assert not hasattr(result, "unknown_field")

    @pytest.mark.parametrize("invalid_limit", [0, -100, 10001, "abc", None, 3.14])
    def test_invalid_limit_values(self, invalid_limit):
        with pytest.raises((ValueError, TypeError)):
            validate_input({"query": "test", "limit": invalid_limit})

Notice the parametrize decorator on the last test. The pipeline rewards parametrized tests because they efficiently cover multiple edge cases. A single parametrized test with six cases counts more toward your edge-case score than six separate tests with one case each.

Testing Core Logic

Your skill's core transformation or computation logic should be tested thoroughly with known inputs and expected outputs:

class TestResultFormatter:
    """Tests for formatting skill output."""

    def test_formats_single_result(self):
        raw = [{"name": "tool-a", "score": 0.95, "description": "A tool"}]
        formatted = format_results(raw)
        assert len(formatted) == 1
        assert formatted[0]["name"] == "tool-a"
        assert formatted[0]["relevance"] == "95%"

    def test_sorts_by_score_descending(self):
        raw = [
            {"name": "low", "score": 0.3, "description": "Low"},
            {"name": "high", "score": 0.9, "description": "High"},
            {"name": "mid", "score": 0.6, "description": "Mid"},
        ]
        formatted = format_results(raw)
        assert [r["name"] for r in formatted] == ["high", "mid", "low"]

    def test_empty_results_returns_empty_list(self):
        assert format_results([]) == []

    def test_truncates_long_descriptions(self):
        raw = [{"name": "tool", "score": 0.5, "description": "x" * 500}]
        formatted = format_results(raw)
        assert len(formatted[0]["description"]) <= 200

    def test_handles_missing_description(self):
        raw = [{"name": "tool", "score": 0.5}]
        formatted = format_results(raw)
        assert formatted[0]["description"] == "No description available"

Integration Tests: Testing the Full Execution Path

Unit tests verify individual pieces. Integration tests verify that those pieces work together correctly when your skill processes a real request end to end.

class TestSkillExecution:
    """Integration tests for the complete skill execution path."""

    def test_full_execution_returns_valid_output(self, mock_api):
        mock_api.configure({"search": [{"name": "tool-a", "score": 0.9}]})

        result = execute_skill({
            "query": "find Python testing tools",
            "limit": 5
        })

        assert result["status"] == "success"
        assert len(result["results"]) == 1
        assert result["results"][0]["name"] == "tool-a"
        assert "execution_time_ms" in result["metadata"]

    def test_execution_with_no_results(self, mock_api):
        mock_api.configure({"search": []})

        result = execute_skill({"query": "nonexistent tool xyz", "limit": 5})

        assert result["status"] == "success"
        assert result["results"] == []
        assert result["metadata"]["total_count"] == 0

    def test_execution_with_api_failure(self, mock_api):
        mock_api.configure_error(status=503, message="Service unavailable")

        result = execute_skill({"query": "test", "limit": 5})

        assert result["status"] == "error"
        assert "Service unavailable" in result["error"]["message"]
        assert result["error"]["retryable"] is True

The pipeline distinguishes between unit and integration tests. Having both categories, with integration tests covering the happy path and key error scenarios, significantly boosts your score.

Mock Strategies for API-Dependent Tools

Many agent skills call external APIs — LLM providers, databases, third-party services. The verification pipeline runs in a sandboxed environment without network access. If your tests make real API calls, they will fail, and your score drops dramatically.

Using pytest Fixtures for Mocking

# conftest.py
import pytest
from unittest.mock import AsyncMock, MagicMock, patch


@pytest.fixture
def mock_llm_client():
    """Mock the LLM API client."""
    with patch("src.handlers.llm_client") as mock:
        mock.complete = AsyncMock(return_value={
            "text": "Mocked LLM response",
            "tokens_used": 150,
            "model": "test-model"
        })
        yield mock


@pytest.fixture
def mock_database():
    """Mock database connections."""
    with patch("src.handlers.db") as mock:
        mock.query = MagicMock(return_value=[
            {"id": 1, "name": "result-1"},
            {"id": 2, "name": "result-2"},
        ])
        mock.insert = MagicMock(return_value={"id": 3})
        yield mock

Testing with Realistic Mock Data

The pipeline checks whether your mock data is realistic. Returning "test" for every field scores lower than returning data that matches the shape and constraints of real API responses:

@pytest.fixture
def realistic_api_response():
    """Return a response that matches the real API shape."""
    return {
        "results": [
            {
                "id": "pkg_a1b2c3d4",
                "name": "code-analyzer",
                "version": "1.4.2",
                "trust_level": "gold",
                "description": "Static code analysis for Python projects",
                "downloads_30d": 12450,
                "created_at": "2025-11-15T08:30:00Z",
                "tags": ["python", "static-analysis", "code-quality"]
            }
        ],
        "total": 1,
        "page": 1,
        "per_page": 20
    }

Mocking with Side Effects

For skills that retry on failure or handle rate limits, use side effects to simulate sequences of responses:

def test_retries_on_transient_failure(self, mock_llm_client):
    mock_llm_client.complete = AsyncMock(
        side_effect=[
            ConnectionError("timeout"),
            ConnectionError("timeout"),
            {"text": "Success on third try", "tokens_used": 50}
        ]
    )

    result = await execute_with_retry({"prompt": "test"}, max_retries=3)

    assert result["text"] == "Success on third try"
    assert mock_llm_client.complete.call_count == 3

What the Verification Pipeline Checks

Here is the complete checklist the pipeline evaluates. Meeting all of these criteria is what separates Gold from Silver:

Coverage Checks

  • Line coverage above 80% (required for Silver, 90%+ for Gold)
  • Branch coverage above 70% (required for Gold)
  • All public functions have at least one test
  • Error handling paths are covered

Correctness Checks

  • Tests contain meaningful assertions (not just assert True)
  • Return values are checked against expected outputs
  • Error types and messages are verified, not just that exceptions are raised
  • Output schemas match the declared ANP specification

Edge-Case Checks

  • Empty inputs are tested
  • Boundary values are tested (zero, max int, empty string, very long string)
  • Invalid types are tested
  • Concurrent execution is tested (if applicable)

Determinism Checks

  • Tests do not depend on current time, random values, or execution order
  • Tests do not depend on network access
  • Tests do not write to the filesystem without cleanup
  • All external dependencies are mocked

For the full specifications on how verification integrates with publishing, see the testing guide for agent skills.

Tips for Reaching Gold Tier

Gold tier requires a verification score of 90 or above. Here are the patterns that consistently push skills past that threshold:

1. Use Parametrized Tests Aggressively

@pytest.mark.parametrize("input_text,expected_lang", [
    ("Hello world", "en"),
    ("Bonjour le monde", "fr"),
    ("Hola mundo", "es"),
    ("", "unknown"),
    ("12345", "unknown"),
    ("Hello Bonjour mixed", "en"),  # defaults to first detected
])
def test_language_detection(input_text, expected_lang):
    result = detect_language(input_text)
    assert result == expected_lang

2. Test the Output Schema Explicitly

def test_output_matches_anp_schema(self, mock_api):
    mock_api.configure({"search": sample_results})
    result = execute_skill({"query": "test", "limit": 5})

    # Verify all required fields exist
    assert "status" in result
    assert "results" in result
    assert "metadata" in result

    # Verify field types
    assert isinstance(result["status"], str)
    assert isinstance(result["results"], list)
    assert isinstance(result["metadata"], dict)

    # Verify metadata fields
    assert "execution_time_ms" in result["metadata"]
    assert isinstance(result["metadata"]["execution_time_ms"], (int, float))
    assert result["metadata"]["execution_time_ms"] >= 0

3. Test Error Messages, Not Just Error Types

# Bad — only checks type
def test_invalid_input_bad():
    with pytest.raises(ValueError):
        validate_input({})

# Good — checks type AND message
def test_invalid_input_good():
    with pytest.raises(ValueError, match="query is required"):
        validate_input({})

4. Add Cleanup to Filesystem Tests

@pytest.fixture
def temp_output_dir(tmp_path):
    """Provide a temporary directory that is cleaned up automatically."""
    output_dir = tmp_path / "output"
    output_dir.mkdir()
    yield output_dir
    # tmp_path is cleaned up automatically by pytest

def test_writes_output_file(temp_output_dir):
    result = export_results([{"name": "tool"}], output_dir=str(temp_output_dir))
    output_file = temp_output_dir / "results.json"
    assert output_file.exists()
    assert json.loads(output_file.read_text()) == [{"name": "tool"}]

5. Freeze Time for Timestamp-Dependent Logic

from freezegun import freeze_time

@freeze_time("2026-03-15T10:00:00Z")
def test_adds_timestamp_to_output():
    result = execute_skill({"query": "test"})
    assert result["metadata"]["timestamp"] == "2026-03-15T10:00:00Z"

Common Pitfalls That Tank Your Score

These are the mistakes that most frequently prevent skills from reaching Gold:

Pitfall 1: Tests That Only Check Happy Path

If all your tests pass valid input and check for success, you miss the edge-case and error-handling dimensions entirely. Aim for at least 30% of your tests to cover error and boundary conditions.

Pitfall 2: Assertions on Implementation Details

Testing that a specific internal function was called a specific number of times creates brittle tests. Instead, assert on observable outputs. The pipeline penalizes tests that break when internal implementation changes without affecting behavior.

Pitfall 3: Non-Deterministic Tests

Tests that use random.random(), datetime.now(), or uuid.uuid4() without seeding or mocking will produce different results across runs. The pipeline runs your tests multiple times and flags any inconsistency.

Pitfall 4: Missing conftest.py

Without shared fixtures in conftest.py, you end up duplicating mock setup across test files. The pipeline does not penalize duplication directly, but duplicated setup tends to drift and create inconsistencies that hurt your determinism score.

Pitfall 5: Skipping Async Tests

If your skill uses async functions but your tests only cover synchronous wrappers, you miss code paths. Use pytest-asyncio for async test support:

import pytest

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_async_execution():
    result = await execute_skill_async({"query": "test"})
    assert result["status"] == "success"

Putting It All Together

Here is a complete test file for a skill that searches for packages, demonstrating all the patterns discussed:

import pytest
from unittest.mock import AsyncMock, patch
from freezegun import freeze_time
from src.main import execute_skill
from src.handlers import validate_input


@pytest.fixture
def mock_search_api():
    with patch("src.handlers.search_api") as mock:
        mock.search = AsyncMock(return_value={
            "results": [{"name": "tool-a", "score": 0.9, "trust_level": "gold"}],
            "total": 1
        })
        yield mock


class TestValidation:
    def test_valid_input(self):
        result = validate_input({"query": "python tools", "limit": 10})
        assert result.query == "python tools"

    @pytest.mark.parametrize("bad_input,error_msg", [
        ({}, "query is required"),
        ({"query": ""}, "query must not be empty"),
        ({"query": "x" * 1001}, "query exceeds maximum length"),
        ({"query": "ok", "limit": -1}, "limit must be positive"),
        ({"query": "ok", "limit": 0}, "limit must be positive"),
    ])
    def test_invalid_inputs(self, bad_input, error_msg):
        with pytest.raises(ValueError, match=error_msg):
            validate_input(bad_input)


class TestExecution:
    @pytest.mark.asyncio
    @freeze_time("2026-03-15T10:00:00Z")
    async def test_successful_search(self, mock_search_api):
        result = await execute_skill({"query": "python tools", "limit": 5})
        assert result["status"] == "success"
        assert len(result["results"]) == 1
        assert result["metadata"]["timestamp"] == "2026-03-15T10:00:00Z"

    @pytest.mark.asyncio
    async def test_empty_results(self, mock_search_api):
        mock_search_api.search.return_value = {"results": [], "total": 0}
        result = await execute_skill({"query": "nonexistent", "limit": 5})
        assert result["status"] == "success"
        assert result["results"] == []

    @pytest.mark.asyncio
    async def test_api_failure(self, mock_search_api):
        mock_search_api.search.side_effect = ConnectionError("timeout")
        result = await execute_skill({"query": "test", "limit": 5})
        assert result["status"] == "error"
        assert result["error"]["retryable"] is True

Ready to publish and verify your skill? Make sure your test suite covers the five dimensions, follows the patterns in this guide, and avoids the common pitfalls. If you are building a skill for the first time, the guide on how to publish your ANP package walks you through the full process from project setup to live listing.

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I write tests for agent skills?

Write tests using pytest in a tests/ directory at your project root. Each source module should have a corresponding test file. Include unit tests for input validation and core logic, and integration tests for the full execution path. Mock all external API calls using pytest fixtures and unittest.mock. Use pytest-asyncio for async functions and freezegun for timestamp-dependent logic.

What is a good verification score?

A score of 70 or above earns Silver tier, and 90 or above earns Gold. The score is a weighted average across five dimensions: coverage, correctness, edge-case handling, determinism, and isolation. Most skills land in the 50-70 range on their first submission. Focusing on parametrized edge-case tests and explicit output schema assertions is the fastest path to improvement.

How do I reach Gold tier?

Gold tier requires 90% or higher line coverage, 70% branch coverage, meaningful assertions on all outputs, comprehensive edge-case testing, and fully deterministic test execution. Use parametrized tests for edge cases, freeze time-dependent logic, mock all external dependencies, and verify error messages — not just error types. Test both the happy path and at least three error scenarios for each public function.

Do I need tests to publish on AgentNode?

You can publish without tests, but your skill will receive a Bronze tier rating at best. Skills without tests cannot achieve Silver or Gold, which means they appear lower in search results and show a lower trust signal to consumers. In practice, most developers and AI agents filter for Silver or Gold, so untested skills receive significantly less usage.

title: MCP vs ANP: Two Standards for AI Agent Tools Compared
excerpt: A technical comparison of MCP (Model Context Protocol) and ANP (AgentNode Package) — what each standard solves, their architectural differences, compatibility story, and when to use which.
seo_title: MCP vs ANP: AI Agent Tool Standards Compared (2026)
seo_description: Technical comparison of MCP and ANP standards for AI agent tools. Architecture, compatibility, when to use each, and how AgentNode bridges both.
content_html:
The AI agent ecosystem now has two prominent standards for tool integration: MCP (Model Context Protocol) and ANP (AgentNode Package). If you are building agent tools or choosing a standard for your team, you have probably encountered both and wondered how they differ, whether they compete, and which one you should adopt.

The short answer: they solve different problems and work well together. MCP defines how an AI model connects to a tool server at runtime. ANP defines how a tool is packaged, distributed, verified, and discovered. This article provides a thorough technical comparison so you can make an informed decision.

What Is MCP (Model Context Protocol)?

MCP — the Model Context Protocol — is an open standard originally developed by Anthropic that defines a communication protocol between AI models and tool servers. Think of it as the USB standard for AI tools: it specifies the shape of the plug, the handshake protocol, and the data format that flows through the connection.

MCP Architecture

MCP uses a client-server architecture:

  • MCP Client — Runs inside the AI model's runtime (Claude, Cursor, an IDE plugin)
  • MCP Server — A process that exposes tools, resources, and prompts over a standardized protocol
  • Transport — Communication happens over stdio, HTTP/SSE, or WebSocket

When an AI model needs to use a tool, the MCP client sends a JSON-RPC request to the MCP server. The server executes the tool and returns results through the same channel.

// MCP tool definition (simplified)
{
  "name": "search_files",
  "description": "Search for files matching a pattern",
  "inputSchema": {
    "type": "object",
    "properties": {
      "pattern": { "type": "string" },
      "directory": { "type": "string" }
    },
    "required": ["pattern"]
  }
}

What MCP Solves

  • Standardized runtime communication between AI models and tools
  • Tool discovery within a running server session
  • Resource access (files, database rows, API responses)
  • Prompt templates that tools can expose to models
  • Security boundaries through capability negotiation

For a hands-on walkthrough of MCP in action, see the tutorial on using MCP with AgentNode in Claude and Cursor.

What Is ANP (AgentNode Package)?

ANP — the AgentNode Package standard — defines how agent tools are packaged, distributed, verified, and discovered. If MCP is the USB plug, ANP is the app store: it handles everything that happens before and after the runtime connection.

ANP Architecture

ANP operates at the package level:

  • Package manifest (anp.yaml) — Declares the tool's name, version, inputs, outputs, dependencies, and supported frameworks
  • Verification pipeline — Automated testing and security analysis that assigns a trust tier (Bronze, Silver, Gold)
  • Registry — A searchable catalog where verified packages are published and discovered
  • SDK — Client libraries for installing and loading tools in Python, TypeScript, and other languages
# ANP manifest (anp.yaml)
name: search-files
version: 2.1.0
description: Search for files matching a pattern across directories
author: developer-handle
license: MIT

inputs:
  pattern:
    type: string
    required: true
    description: Glob pattern to match
  directory:
    type: string
    default: "."
    description: Root directory for search

outputs:
  matches:
    type: array
    items:
      type: string
    description: List of matching file paths
  count:
    type: integer
    description: Number of matches found

frameworks:
  - mcp
  - langchain
  - crewai

What ANP Solves

  • Packaging and versioning of agent tools
  • Cross-framework compatibility (one package works with MCP, LangChain, CrewAI, and others)
  • Verification and trust scoring
  • Discovery through a searchable registry
  • Dependency management between tools

For a deeper exploration of the standard itself, read the ANP deep dive or the tutorial on the ANP standard explained.

Head-to-Head Comparison

Here is a detailed comparison across the dimensions that matter most when choosing a standard:

Scope and Purpose

  • MCP — Runtime protocol. Defines how AI models talk to tools while executing.
  • ANP — Package standard. Defines how tools are built, tested, distributed, and discovered.

These scopes are complementary, not overlapping. A tool can be packaged as an ANP package and expose itself as an MCP server at runtime.

Framework Support

  • MCP — Natively supported by Claude, Cursor, Windsurf, and a growing number of IDE-integrated AI assistants. Requires an MCP client in the consuming application.
  • ANP — Framework-agnostic. A single ANP package can generate runtime adapters for MCP, LangChain, CrewAI, AutoGen, Semantic Kernel, and others. The consumer does not need to know the package was built with ANP.

Distribution Model

  • MCP — Tools are distributed as server implementations. Users install and run the server locally or connect to a remote instance. No centralized registry (though community lists exist).
  • ANP — Tools are distributed through the AgentNode registry with versioning, verification, and searchable metadata. Similar to npm for Node.js packages.

Trust and Verification

  • MCP — No built-in verification. Trust depends on the source of the server implementation. Users must audit code themselves or trust the author.
  • ANP — Built-in verification pipeline that analyzes tests, scans for vulnerabilities, and assigns trust tiers. Every package version is independently verified.

Discovery

  • MCP — Tools are discovered within a running server session. The server declares what tools it offers when a client connects. No cross-server discovery mechanism.
  • ANP — Tools are discovered through the registry before installation. Agents and developers can search by keyword, category, framework, and trust tier.

Versioning

  • MCP — Protocol itself is versioned. Individual tool versions depend on the server implementation's deployment strategy.
  • ANP — Semantic versioning at the package level. Multiple versions can coexist in the registry. Consumers can pin specific versions.

Not Competing — Complementary

The most important insight in this comparison is that MCP and ANP are not competing standards. They operate at different layers of the stack:

┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│         AI Model / Agent Runtime         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│         MCP (Runtime Protocol)           │  ← How tools communicate
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│      ANP (Package & Distribution)        │  ← How tools are built & shared
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│        Tool Implementation Code          │
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘

A typical workflow using both standards looks like this:

  1. A developer builds a tool and packages it as an ANP package
  2. The ANP package includes an MCP adapter in its framework declarations
  3. The package is published to the AgentNode registry and verified
  4. A consumer discovers the package through the registry
  5. At runtime, the tool communicates with the AI model over MCP

AgentNode bridges both standards automatically. When you install an ANP package and your runtime uses MCP, the SDK generates the appropriate MCP server wrapper. You get ANP's packaging, verification, and discovery benefits while using MCP's runtime protocol.

When to Use Which Standard

Here are practical guidelines for choosing based on your situation:

Use MCP When:

  • You are building a tool specifically for Claude, Cursor, or another MCP-native client
  • Your tool needs to expose resources and prompts alongside callable functions
  • You are integrating with a local development environment where the tool runs as a sidecar process
  • You do not need cross-framework distribution

Use ANP When:

  • You want your tool to work across multiple frameworks (MCP, LangChain, CrewAI, etc.)
  • You want automated verification and trust scoring
  • You want your tool discoverable in a searchable registry
  • You are building tools for a team or community that uses diverse agent frameworks

Use Both When:

  • You want the widest possible reach — package with ANP, expose via MCP at runtime
  • You want verification and trust without sacrificing MCP compatibility
  • Your consumers include both MCP-native clients and SDK-based integrations

How AgentNode Bridges Both Standards

AgentNode was designed from the ground up to be standard-agnostic at the runtime layer while providing opinionated packaging through ANP. Here is how the bridging works in practice:

from agentnode_sdk import AgentNode

client = AgentNode()

# Install an ANP package
client.install("code-analyzer")

# Load it as a Python callable
tool = load_tool("code-analyzer")
result = tool.run({"code": source_code})

# Or start it as an MCP server
from agentnode.mcp import serve_as_mcp
serve_as_mcp("code-analyzer", transport="stdio")

The serve_as_mcp function takes any ANP package and wraps it in a compliant MCP server. The tool's ANP manifest provides the input/output schemas, and the MCP adapter translates them into MCP tool definitions automatically.

For the full technical documentation on bridging, visit the technical documentation.

The Convergence Trend

Looking at the broader ecosystem, we are seeing convergence rather than fragmentation. MCP has become the dominant runtime protocol for AI-to-tool communication. ANP has become the leading packaging standard for cross-framework distribution. Other frameworks are increasingly adopting one or both.

LangChain added MCP client support in late 2025. CrewAI followed in early 2026. Google's ADK supports MCP servers natively. On the packaging side, tools distributed through ANP can target all of these frameworks from a single codebase.

This convergence is good for everyone. Tool authors write once and distribute everywhere. Tool consumers get a consistent discovery and trust model regardless of their framework choice. And AI models get access to a growing catalog of verified, interoperable tools.

Migration Guide

If you have existing tools in one standard and want to adopt the other:

MCP Server to ANP Package

  1. Create an anp.yaml manifest with your tool's metadata, inputs, and outputs
  2. Move your tool logic into a framework-agnostic module
  3. Add mcp to the frameworks list in your manifest
  4. Write tests (required for Silver/Gold verification)
  5. Publish to the AgentNode registry

ANP Package to MCP Server

  1. Your ANP package already works — use serve_as_mcp() to expose it
  2. If your manifest includes mcp in frameworks, the adapter is generated automatically
  3. Configure transport (stdio for local, HTTP/SSE for remote)

The tutorial on building agent skills with Builder walks through creating an ANP package from scratch, including MCP compatibility.

Summary Table

For quick reference, here are the key differences at a glance:

  • Layer — MCP: Runtime protocol | ANP: Package standard
  • Primary function — MCP: AI-to-tool communication | ANP: Tool packaging, distribution, discovery
  • Registry — MCP: None (community lists) | ANP: AgentNode registry
  • Verification — MCP: None built-in | ANP: Automated pipeline with trust tiers
  • Framework support — MCP: MCP-compatible clients | ANP: Cross-framework (MCP, LangChain, CrewAI, etc.)
  • Versioning — MCP: Protocol-level | ANP: Package-level semver
  • Discovery — MCP: Within server session | ANP: Registry-wide search
  • Relationship — Complementary — ANP packages can expose MCP interfaces at runtime

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the difference between MCP and ANP?

MCP (Model Context Protocol) is a runtime communication protocol that defines how AI models talk to tool servers during execution. ANP (AgentNode Package) is a packaging and distribution standard that defines how tools are built, verified, versioned, and discovered. MCP handles the runtime connection; ANP handles everything before and after — packaging, testing, publishing, and discovery.

Are MCP and ANP competing standards?

No. MCP and ANP operate at different layers of the stack and are complementary. A tool can be packaged as an ANP package and communicate via MCP at runtime. AgentNode bridges both standards automatically, generating MCP server wrappers for ANP packages when needed.

Can I use both MCP and ANP?

Yes, and this is the recommended approach for maximum reach. Package your tool as an ANP package with mcp listed in the frameworks field. This gives you ANP's verification, versioning, and discovery benefits while maintaining full MCP compatibility. The AgentNode SDK handles the bridging automatically.

Which standard should I choose?

If you only need your tool to work with MCP-native clients like Claude or Cursor, MCP alone is sufficient. If you want cross-framework distribution, automated verification, and registry-based discovery, use ANP. For the widest possible reach, use both — ANP for packaging and distribution, MCP for runtime communication.

title: How to Build a Code Review Agent with AI Tools
excerpt: Build a code review agent that uses AgentNode tools for static analysis, style checking, security scanning, and generating review comments. Includes architecture, tool selection, code walkthrough, and GitHub PR integration.
seo_title: Build a Code Review Agent with AI Tools | Tutorial
seo_description: Step-by-step guide to building an AI code review agent. Static analysis, security scanning, style checks, and GitHub PR integration with AgentNode.
content_html:
Code review is one of the most time-consuming tasks in software development. A thorough review requires checking for bugs, security vulnerabilities, style violations, and logical errors — all while understanding the broader context of the change. AI agents are uniquely suited for this work because they can apply multiple analysis tools in parallel and synthesize findings into actionable feedback.

This tutorial walks you through building a code review agent that uses AgentNode tools for static analysis, style checking, security scanning, and generating human-readable review comments. By the end, you will have an agent that can review pull requests on GitHub and post its findings as inline comments.

Architecture Overview

The code review agent follows a pipeline architecture with four stages:

  1. Diff extraction — Pull the changed files and diffs from a GitHub pull request
  2. Parallel analysis — Run static analysis, style checking, and security scanning concurrently
  3. Synthesis — Combine findings from all analyzers and generate human-readable comments
  4. Posting — Submit inline review comments on the pull request
┌──────────────┐
│  GitHub PR   │
└──────┬───────┘
       │ Extract diff
       ▼
┌──────────────┐     ┌─────────────┐     ┌────────────────┐
│   Static     │     │   Style     │     │   Security     │
│  Analysis    │     │  Checker    │     │   Scanner      │
└──────┬───────┘     └──────┬──────┘     └───────┬────────┘
       │                    │                     │
       └──────────┬─────────┘─────────────────────┘
                  │ Merge findings
                  ▼
         ┌────────────────┐
         │   Comment      │
         │  Synthesizer   │
         └────────┬───────┘
                  │ Post comments
                  ▼
         ┌────────────────┐
         │  GitHub API    │
         └────────────────┘

Each analysis stage is powered by a verified AgentNode tool. The synthesis stage uses an LLM tool to convert raw findings into helpful, contextual review comments.

Selecting Tools from AgentNode

The first step is choosing the right tools for each analysis stage. You can browse code analysis agent tools on AgentNode to see the full catalog. For this tutorial, we will use four tools:

1. code-static-analyzer

Performs AST-level static analysis for Python, JavaScript, and TypeScript. Detects unused variables, unreachable code, type mismatches, and potential null reference errors.

2. style-linter

Checks code against configurable style rules. Supports PEP 8, Airbnb JavaScript, Google TypeScript, and custom rule sets.

3. security-scanner

Scans for common security vulnerabilities: SQL injection, XSS, hardcoded secrets, insecure dependencies, and OWASP Top 10 patterns.

4. comment-synthesizer

Takes raw analysis findings and generates human-readable review comments with explanations, severity levels, and suggested fixes.

from agentnode_sdk import AgentNode

client = AgentNode()

# Install all required tools
client.install("code-static-analyzer")
client.install("style-linter")
client.install("security-scanner")
client.install("comment-synthesizer")

Step 1: Extract the Diff from GitHub

The agent starts by fetching the pull request diff from GitHub. We use the GitHub API directly rather than an agent tool for this step, since it is straightforward HTTP:

import httpx
from dataclasses import dataclass


@dataclass
class PRFile:
    filename: str
    status: str  # added, modified, removed
    patch: str   # unified diff
    content: str # full file content (new version)


async def extract_pr_files(
    owner: str, repo: str, pr_number: int, token: str
) -> list[PRFile]:
    """Fetch changed files from a GitHub pull request."""
    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as http:
        headers = {
            "Authorization": f"Bearer {token}",
            "Accept": "application/vnd.github.v3+json"
        }

        # Get list of changed files
        resp = await http.get(
            f"https://api.github.com/repos/{owner}/{repo}/pulls/{pr_number}/files",
            headers=headers
        )
        resp.raise_for_status()
        files_data = resp.json()

        pr_files = []
        for f in files_data:
            if f["status"] == "removed":
                continue  # skip deleted files

            # Fetch full file content
            content_resp = await http.get(
                f["raw_url"], headers=headers
            )
            content = content_resp.text if content_resp.status_code == 200 else ""

            pr_files.append(PRFile(
                filename=f["filename"],
                status=f["status"],
                patch=f.get("patch", ""),
                content=content
            ))

        return pr_files

Step 2: Run Parallel Analysis

With the files extracted, we run all three analysis tools concurrently on each file. This is where agent tools shine — each tool is independent, so we can maximize throughput with asyncio.gather():

import asyncio
from agentnode_sdk import AgentNode

client = AgentNode()
static_analyzer = load_tool("code-static-analyzer")
style_linter = load_tool("style-linter")
security_scanner = load_tool("security-scanner")


async def analyze_file(pr_file: PRFile) -> dict:
    """Run all analyzers on a single file concurrently."""
    # Determine language from file extension
    lang = detect_language(pr_file.filename)
    if lang is None:
        return {"filename": pr_file.filename, "findings": []}

    # Run all three analyzers in parallel
    static_task = static_analyzer.arun({
        "code": pr_file.content,
        "language": lang,
        "severity_threshold": "warning"
    })

    style_task = style_linter.arun({
        "code": pr_file.content,
        "language": lang,
        "ruleset": get_ruleset(lang),
        "fix_suggestions": True
    })

    security_task = security_scanner.arun({
        "code": pr_file.content,
        "language": lang,
        "scan_mode": "diff",
        "diff": pr_file.patch
    })

    static_result, style_result, security_result = await asyncio.gather(
        static_task, style_task, security_task,
        return_exceptions=True
    )

    # Collect findings from all analyzers
    findings = []

    if not isinstance(static_result, Exception):
        for issue in static_result.output.get("issues", []):
            findings.append({
                "source": "static-analysis",
                "line": issue["line"],
                "severity": issue["severity"],
                "message": issue["message"],
                "rule": issue["rule_id"]
            })

    if not isinstance(style_result, Exception):
        for violation in style_result.output.get("violations", []):
            findings.append({
                "source": "style",
                "line": violation["line"],
                "severity": "info",
                "message": violation["message"],
                "suggestion": violation.get("fix")
            })

    if not isinstance(security_result, Exception):
        for vuln in security_result.output.get("vulnerabilities", []):
            findings.append({
                "source": "security",
                "line": vuln["line"],
                "severity": vuln["severity"],
                "message": vuln["description"],
                "cwe": vuln.get("cwe_id"),
                "owasp": vuln.get("owasp_category")
            })

    return {"filename": pr_file.filename, "findings": findings}


def detect_language(filename: str) -> str | None:
    """Map file extension to language identifier."""
    ext_map = {
        ".py": "python",
        ".js": "javascript",
        ".ts": "typescript",
        ".tsx": "typescript",
        ".jsx": "javascript",
    }
    for ext, lang in ext_map.items():
        if filename.endswith(ext):
            return lang
    return None


def get_ruleset(language: str) -> str:
    """Get the default style ruleset for a language."""
    return {
        "python": "pep8",
        "javascript": "airbnb",
        "typescript": "google-ts",
    }.get(language, "default")

Step 3: Synthesize Review Comments

Raw analysis findings are technical and terse. The comment synthesizer transforms them into helpful review comments that explain the issue, its impact, and how to fix it:

synthesizer = load_tool("comment-synthesizer")


async def synthesize_comments(analysis_results: list[dict]) -> list[dict]:
    """Convert raw findings into human-readable review comments."""
    all_comments = []

    for file_result in analysis_results:
        if not file_result["findings"]:
            continue

        # Group findings by line to create consolidated comments
        by_line = {}
        for finding in file_result["findings"]:
            line = finding["line"]
            if line not in by_line:
                by_line[line] = []
            by_line[line].append(finding)

        for line, findings in by_line.items():
            # Use the synthesizer to generate a readable comment
            result = await synthesizer.arun({
                "findings": findings,
                "filename": file_result["filename"],
                "line": line,
                "style": "constructive",  # constructive, not adversarial
                "include_fix": True
            })

            comment = result.output
            all_comments.append({
                "path": file_result["filename"],
                "line": line,
                "body": comment["markdown"],
                "severity": comment["max_severity"]
            })

    # Sort by severity (critical first)
    severity_order = {"critical": 0, "error": 1, "warning": 2, "info": 3}
    all_comments.sort(key=lambda c: severity_order.get(c["severity"], 4))

    return all_comments

Step 4: Post Comments to GitHub

The final step posts the synthesized comments as an inline pull request review on GitHub:

async def post_review(
    owner: str, repo: str, pr_number: int,
    comments: list[dict], token: str
) -> dict:
    """Post review comments on a GitHub pull request."""
    # Build the review payload
    review_comments = []
    for comment in comments:
        review_comments.append({
            "path": comment["path"],
            "line": comment["line"],
            "body": comment["body"]
        })

    # Determine overall review action
    has_critical = any(c["severity"] in ("critical", "error") for c in comments)
    event = "REQUEST_CHANGES" if has_critical else "COMMENT"

    # Build summary
    summary_parts = []
    severity_counts = {}
    for c in comments:
        severity_counts[c["severity"]] = severity_counts.get(c["severity"], 0) + 1
    for sev in ["critical", "error", "warning", "info"]:
        if sev in severity_counts:
            summary_parts.append(f"{severity_counts[sev]} {sev}")

    summary = f"Automated review found {', '.join(summary_parts)} issue(s)."
    if not comments:
        summary = "Automated review found no issues. Looks good!"
        event = "APPROVE"

    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as http:
        resp = await http.post(
            f"https://api.github.com/repos/{owner}/{repo}/pulls/{pr_number}/reviews",
            headers={
                "Authorization": f"Bearer {token}",
                "Accept": "application/vnd.github.v3+json"
            },
            json={
                "body": summary,
                "event": event,
                "comments": review_comments
            }
        )
        resp.raise_for_status()
        return resp.json()

Putting It All Together

Here is the complete agent that ties all four stages together:

import asyncio
from agentnode_sdk import AgentNode


async def review_pull_request(
    owner: str, repo: str, pr_number: int, github_token: str
) -> dict:
    """Run a complete AI code review on a pull request."""

    # Stage 1: Extract files
    pr_files = await extract_pr_files(owner, repo, pr_number, github_token)
    print(f"Extracted {len(pr_files)} changed files")

    # Stage 2: Analyze all files in parallel
    analysis_tasks = [analyze_file(f) for f in pr_files]
    analysis_results = await asyncio.gather(*analysis_tasks)

    total_findings = sum(len(r["findings"]) for r in analysis_results)
    print(f"Found {total_findings} issues across all analyzers")

    # Stage 3: Synthesize comments
    comments = await synthesize_comments(analysis_results)
    print(f"Generated {len(comments)} review comments")

    # Stage 4: Post review
    if comments or True:  # always post, even if no issues
        review = await post_review(
            owner, repo, pr_number, comments, github_token
        )
        print(f"Posted review: {review['html_url']}")
        return review


# Run the agent
if __name__ == "__main__":
    asyncio.run(review_pull_request(
        owner="your-org",
        repo="your-repo",
        pr_number=42,
        github_token="ghp_your_token"
    ))

Configuring Analysis Depth

Not every pull request needs the same level of scrutiny. You can configure the agent to adjust its analysis based on the PR's characteristics:

def get_analysis_config(pr_files: list[PRFile]) -> dict:
    """Adjust analysis depth based on PR size and file types."""
    total_lines = sum(len(f.patch.splitlines()) for f in pr_files)
    has_security_files = any(
        f.filename in ("auth.py", "security.py", "middleware.py")
        or "auth" in f.filename.lower()
        for f in pr_files
    )

    config = {
        "severity_threshold": "warning",
        "security_scan_mode": "standard",
        "style_check": True,
    }

    if total_lines > 500:
        # Large PRs: focus on critical issues only
        config["severity_threshold"] = "error"
        config["style_check"] = False

    if has_security_files:
        # Security-sensitive files: deep scan
        config["security_scan_mode"] = "deep"
        config["severity_threshold"] = "info"

    return config

Adding CI/CD Integration

To run the code review agent automatically on every pull request, add it to your GitHub Actions workflow:

# .github/workflows/ai-review.yml
name: AI Code Review

on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, synchronize]

jobs:
  ai-review:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: "3.11"

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: |
          pip install agentnode-sdk httpx
          agentnode install code-static-analyzer
          agentnode install style-linter
          agentnode install security-scanner
          agentnode install comment-synthesizer

      - name: Run AI review
        env:
          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
          AGENTNODE_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.AGENTNODE_API_KEY }}
        run: |
          python review_agent.py \
            --owner ${{ github.repository_owner }} \
            --repo ${{ github.event.repository.name }} \
            --pr ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}

Extending the Agent

This base agent can be extended in many directions. Here are ideas for next steps:

  • Test coverage analysis — Add a tool that checks whether new code has corresponding tests
  • Performance profiling — Flag potentially slow code patterns (N+1 queries, unbounded loops)
  • Documentation checking — Verify that public functions have docstrings and that they match the implementation
  • Dependency auditing — Check new imports against known vulnerability databases

Each extension follows the same pattern: install a verified tool from AgentNode, add it to the parallel analysis stage, and include its findings in the synthesis step. To learn more about building autonomous agents that select their own tools, read about how to build autonomous AI agents.

If you want to create your own analysis tool and publish it for others to use, the guide on how to build agent skills with Builder covers the full process. For the complete SDK reference, visit the AgentNode SDK documentation.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can AI agents review code?

Yes. AI agents can perform static analysis, style checking, security scanning, and generate contextual review comments on pull requests. They work best as a complement to human reviewers — catching mechanical issues like unused variables, style violations, and known vulnerability patterns so human reviewers can focus on architecture, design, and business logic. The agent in this tutorial combines four specialized tools to cover multiple analysis dimensions in parallel.

What tools does a code review agent need?

A comprehensive code review agent needs at minimum three types of tools: a static analyzer for bug detection (unused variables, type errors, unreachable code), a security scanner for vulnerability patterns (SQL injection, XSS, hardcoded secrets), and a comment synthesizer to convert raw findings into helpful review comments. Optional additions include a style linter, test coverage checker, and performance profiler. All of these are available as verified tools on AgentNode.

How to integrate AI code review with GitHub?

Use the GitHub pull request API to extract changed files and diffs, run your analysis tools on the extracted code, and post findings as inline review comments using the pull request reviews endpoint. Automate this by adding the agent to a GitHub Actions workflow that triggers on pull request events. The agent posts its review as a GitHub review with inline comments on specific lines, so developers see feedback directly in the PR interface.

title: State of AI Agent Frameworks in 2026: The Complete Overview
excerpt: A comprehensive overview of the AI agent framework landscape in 2026. Covers LangChain, CrewAI, AutoGen, Google ADK, OpenAI Assistants, Anthropic MCP, Semantic Kernel, DSPy — market trends, what is growing, and the convergence on tool standards.
seo_title: State of AI Agent Frameworks 2026: Complete Overview
seo_description: The AI agent framework landscape in 2026: LangChain, CrewAI, AutoGen, Google ADK, and more. Market trends, growth areas, and tool standard convergence.
content_html:
The AI agent framework landscape has changed dramatically over the past two years. What started as a handful of experimental projects has become a mature ecosystem with clear leaders, emerging challengers, and a surprising trend toward convergence on shared standards. If you are choosing a framework for a new project — or evaluating whether to switch from your current one — this overview gives you the complete picture as of March 2026.

We will cover the eight most significant frameworks, analyze market trends and adoption data, and examine the convergence on tool standards that is reshaping how all of these frameworks interoperate.

The Eight Major Frameworks

These are the frameworks that command meaningful market share, active development, and production usage in 2026.

1. LangChain

LangChain remains the most widely adopted agent framework by install count, though its growth rate has slowed significantly. It pioneered the chain-of-thought abstraction and built a massive ecosystem of integrations.

  • Language: Python, JavaScript/TypeScript
  • Strengths: Largest ecosystem, extensive documentation, strong community
  • Weaknesses: Abstraction complexity, frequent breaking changes in earlier versions (stabilized in v0.3+)
  • Best for: Teams that need the widest range of pre-built integrations
  • Trend: Stable — growth has plateaued as alternatives matured

LangChain's most significant recent move was adding native MCP client support, allowing LangChain agents to consume MCP tool servers directly. This reduced the integration barrier with tools built for Claude and Cursor. For a detailed comparison with its closest competitors, see the framework comparison guide.

2. CrewAI

CrewAI carved out a strong niche with its role-based multi-agent approach. Instead of a single agent with tools, CrewAI lets you define a crew of specialized agents that collaborate on tasks.

  • Language: Python
  • Strengths: Intuitive multi-agent orchestration, role-based design, clean API
  • Weaknesses: Smaller ecosystem than LangChain, Python-only
  • Best for: Multi-agent workflows where different roles handle different aspects of a task
  • Trend: Growing — fastest adoption growth among Python-native frameworks

CrewAI's crew metaphor resonates with teams building complex workflows. A typical crew might include a researcher agent, an analyst agent, and a writer agent — each with specialized tools and instructions.

3. Microsoft AutoGen

AutoGen focuses on multi-agent conversation patterns. Agents communicate through structured conversations, making it well-suited for debate, negotiation, and collaborative problem-solving scenarios.

  • Language: Python, .NET
  • Strengths: Sophisticated conversation patterns, strong enterprise backing, .NET support
  • Weaknesses: Steeper learning curve, heavier runtime
  • Best for: Enterprise environments with complex multi-agent collaboration needs
  • Trend: Steady growth — strong in enterprise, less in startups

4. Google Agent Development Kit (ADK)

Google's ADK launched in late 2025 and quickly gained traction thanks to deep integration with Google Cloud services, Gemini models, and the broader Google ecosystem.

  • Language: Python, JavaScript
  • Strengths: First-class Google Cloud integration, strong model support, built-in observability
  • Weaknesses: Newer ecosystem, tighter coupling to Google infrastructure
  • Best for: Teams already using Google Cloud and Gemini models
  • Trend: Rapid growth — benefiting from Google's developer reach

ADK's standout feature is its native support for MCP servers, allowing agents to consume tools from the broader MCP ecosystem without custom integration code.

5. OpenAI Assistants API

OpenAI's Assistants API is not a framework in the traditional sense — it is a hosted service. But its influence on agent architecture is significant, and many teams treat it as their primary agent runtime.

  • Language: REST API (any language)
  • Strengths: Zero infrastructure management, built-in tool execution, file handling, code interpreter
  • Weaknesses: Vendor lock-in, limited customization, opaque execution
  • Best for: Teams that want the fastest path to a working agent without managing infrastructure
  • Trend: Stable — dominant in prototyping, less in production custom agents

6. Anthropic MCP Ecosystem

MCP is a protocol, not a framework, but the ecosystem built around it has framework-like characteristics. Claude Desktop, Cursor, Windsurf, and a growing number of tools use MCP as their interoperability layer.

  • Language: Protocol-level (implementations in Python, TypeScript, Rust, Go)
  • Strengths: Broad adoption as runtime protocol, clean specification, active development
  • Weaknesses: Not a full framework — you need additional orchestration logic
  • Best for: Tool authors who want maximum compatibility, teams using Claude or Cursor
  • Trend: Rapid growth — becoming the de facto runtime protocol

7. Microsoft Semantic Kernel

Semantic Kernel takes a plugin-based approach to agent capabilities. It is deeply integrated with the .NET ecosystem and Azure services.

  • Language: C#/.NET, Python, Java
  • Strengths: Enterprise-grade, multi-language support, Azure integration
  • Weaknesses: More verbose than Python-first alternatives, smaller community
  • Best for: Enterprise .NET shops, teams needing Java or C# support
  • Trend: Steady — strong in enterprise, limited traction in startups

8. DSPy

DSPy takes a fundamentally different approach: instead of writing prompts, you write declarative programs that compile into optimized prompting strategies. It has gained strong academic adoption and is increasingly used in production.

  • Language: Python
  • Strengths: Programmatic prompt optimization, reproducibility, academic rigor
  • Weaknesses: Steeper learning curve, less intuitive for prompt-first developers
  • Best for: Teams that prioritize systematic optimization over rapid prototyping
  • Trend: Growing — especially in research and ML-heavy teams

Market Trends and Adoption Data

Several clear trends have emerged across the framework landscape in 2026:

Trend 1: Multi-Agent Architectures Are Mainstream

Single-agent designs still dominate simple use cases, but production systems increasingly use multiple specialized agents. CrewAI and AutoGen have both seen adoption growth directly tied to this trend. Even LangChain added LangGraph specifically to support multi-agent workflows.

Trend 2: Protocol Convergence on MCP

The most significant ecosystem shift is the convergence on MCP as the runtime protocol for tool communication. LangChain, CrewAI, Google ADK, and Semantic Kernel all added MCP client support within a twelve-month window. This means a tool built as an MCP server can be consumed by agents in any of these frameworks without custom integration.

Trend 3: The Rise of Tool Registries

As the number of available tools has grown, discoverability has become a bottleneck. Developers cannot use tools they cannot find. This has driven adoption of tool registries — searchable catalogs of verified, documented agent tools. AgentNode's registry is the largest, functioning as the agent tools marketplace for the ecosystem.

Trend 4: Framework Fatigue and Simplification

After a period of framework proliferation, teams are increasingly choosing simpler approaches. Many production systems use a lightweight orchestration layer (often just async Python) with verified tools from a registry, rather than adopting a full framework. The framework provides less value when the tools themselves are standardized and portable.

Trend 5: Enterprise Adoption Is Accelerating

Enterprise adoption of agent frameworks doubled between Q1 2025 and Q1 2026. The main drivers are improved reliability, better observability tooling, and the emergence of trust models (like AgentNode's verification tiers) that give security teams confidence in third-party tools.

Framework Selection Guide

Choosing a framework depends on your specific context. Here are decision criteria organized by team type:

For Startups and Small Teams

Start with CrewAI if you need multi-agent workflows, or use lightweight Python with AgentNode tools if a single agent suffices. Avoid heavyweight frameworks that add abstraction without proportional value. You can always adopt a framework later — verified tools are portable across all of them.

For Enterprise Teams

Semantic Kernel for .NET shops, AutoGen for complex multi-agent scenarios, or Google ADK if you are on Google Cloud. All three offer the observability, security, and governance features enterprise teams require.

For Research and ML Teams

DSPy if systematic prompt optimization is important, LangChain if you need the broadest range of model and tool integrations for experiments.

For Tool Authors

Build framework-agnostic tools packaged as ANP packages. This ensures your tool works across all frameworks. To understand why cross-framework compatibility matters, consider that your potential user base multiplies with each framework you support — and ANP handles this automatically.

The Cross-Framework Layer

The convergence on MCP for runtime communication and ANP for packaging has created what we can call the cross-framework layer. This layer sits between frameworks and tools, providing:

  • Portability — A tool packaged as ANP works with any framework that supports MCP or has an ANP SDK adapter
  • Discovery — Tools are found through the registry regardless of which framework the consumer uses
  • Trust — Verification is framework-independent; a Gold-tier tool is Gold-tier whether consumed from LangChain or CrewAI
  • Versioning — Semantic versioning at the tool level prevents framework upgrades from breaking tool integrations

You can explore tools across all frameworks on AgentNode to see this cross-framework layer in practice. Every tool in the registry lists its compatible frameworks, and most support three or more.

Predictions for the Rest of 2026

Based on current trajectory, here is what we expect to see through the rest of 2026:

  • Further MCP adoption — By end of 2026, MCP support will be table stakes for any serious agent framework
  • Consolidation — Some smaller frameworks will merge or be absorbed; the ecosystem will narrow to 4-5 major players
  • Tool-first development — More teams will start by choosing tools and let that decision inform their framework choice, rather than the reverse
  • Specialized vertical frameworks — Expect frameworks optimized for specific domains (healthcare agents, financial agents, DevOps agents) to emerge
  • Improved observability — Tracing, debugging, and monitoring agent behavior will become significantly better across all frameworks

Getting Started

If you are just entering the agent ecosystem, here is the most practical path:

  1. Start by exploring available tools — explore tools across all frameworks to understand what capabilities exist
  2. Build a simple agent with your preferred language's async runtime and AgentNode tools
  3. Adopt a framework only when your orchestration needs outgrow simple async code
  4. Choose based on your specific requirements (multi-agent? enterprise? research?)

The framework matters less than it used to. With standardized tool protocols and portable packages, switching frameworks is no longer a rewrite — it is a configuration change.

Frequently Asked Questions

What are the top AI agent frameworks in 2026?

The top AI agent frameworks in 2026 are LangChain (largest ecosystem), CrewAI (fastest-growing, multi-agent focus), Microsoft AutoGen (enterprise multi-agent), Google ADK (Google Cloud integration), and DSPy (programmatic optimization). OpenAI Assistants API, Anthropic's MCP ecosystem, and Microsoft Semantic Kernel round out the major players. The landscape has matured significantly, with most frameworks converging on MCP as the runtime protocol for tool communication.

Is LangChain still relevant in 2026?

Yes, LangChain remains the most widely installed agent framework by raw numbers. However, its growth rate has plateaued as alternatives like CrewAI and Google ADK have matured. LangChain's main advantage is its massive integration ecosystem. Its main challenge is abstraction complexity — some teams find simpler approaches (lightweight async Python with verified tools) more productive for straightforward use cases.

What is the fastest growing agent framework?

Among Python-native frameworks, CrewAI has the fastest adoption growth rate, driven by strong demand for multi-agent architectures. Google ADK is growing rapidly overall, benefiting from Google's developer ecosystem and native Gemini integration. In the protocol layer, MCP adoption has been the single fastest-growing trend, with most major frameworks adding MCP client support within a twelve-month window.

Do frameworks matter for tool compatibility?

Less than they used to. The convergence on MCP as a runtime protocol and ANP as a packaging standard means tools are increasingly portable across frameworks. A tool packaged as an ANP package can be consumed from LangChain, CrewAI, AutoGen, Google ADK, and others without modification. Choose your framework based on orchestration needs, not tool availability.
